stem: Let \( f(z) = \dfrac{[[c1]]}{z-([[p1]])} + \dfrac{[[c2]]}{z-([[p2]])} + \dfrac{[[c3]]}{z-([[p3]])} \), and let \( C \) be the positively oriented circle \( |z| = [[R]] \). The poles \( z=[[p1]] \) and \( z=[[p2]] \) lie inside \( C \), while \( z=[[p3]] \) lies outside \( C \). By the residue theorem, compute \( \displaystyle\oint_{C} f(z)\,dz \).
solution: Each term \( \dfrac{c}{z-p} \) has a simple pole at \( z=p \) with residue \( c \) there. By the residue theorem, \( \oint_{C} f(z)\,dz = 2\pi i \) times the sum of residues at poles INSIDE \( C \). Only \( z=[[p1]] \) and \( z=[[p2]] \) are enclosed, so the integral is \( 2\pi i\big(([[c1]])+([[c2]])\big) = [[answer]] \); the pole at \( z=[[p3]] \) is outside \( C \) and contributes nothing.
distractor_rationales: This mistakenly includes the residue at \( z=[[p3]] \), which lies outside \( C \) and should not be counted.
This uses only the excluded pole's residue, forgetting the two enclosed poles entirely.
This swaps which pole is excluded, wrongly including \( z=[[p3]] \) instead of \( z=[[p2]] \).
This uses \( \pi i \) instead of \( 2\pi i \) as the residue-theorem constant.
This has the wrong sign (the contour orientation was reversed).
This is off by \( 2\pi i \) from the correct value.

stem: Let \( f(z) = \dfrac{[[k]]}{(z-([[p1]]))(z-([[p2]]))} \), and let \( C \) be the positively oriented circle \( |z| = [[R]] \). Only the pole \( z=[[p1]] \) lies inside \( C \) (the pole \( z=[[p2]] \) lies outside). Compute \( \displaystyle\oint_{C} f(z)\,dz \).
solution: By partial fractions, the residue of \( f \) at the simple pole \( z=[[p1]] \) is \( \displaystyle\lim_{z\to [[p1]]} (z-([[p1]]))f(z) = \dfrac{[[k]]}{[[p1]]-([[p2]])} \). Since \( z=[[p2]] \) lies outside \( C \) and contributes nothing, the residue theorem gives \( \oint_{C} f(z)\,dz = 2\pi i \cdot \dfrac{[[k]]}{[[p1]]-([[p2]])} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This computes the residue at \( z=[[p1]] \) with the denominator terms swapped, giving the wrong sign.
This forgets to divide by \( p_1-p_2 \) entirely, as if the pole were simple with residue \( k \).
This uses \( \pi i \) instead of \( 2\pi i \) as the residue-theorem constant.
This has the wrong sign (the contour orientation was reversed).
This is off by \( 2\pi i \) from the correct value.
This halves the correct value, as if only part of the residue were counted.

stem: Let \( f(z) = \dfrac{[[a]]}{z-([[z0]])} + \dfrac{[[b]]}{(z-([[z0]]))^{2}} + \dfrac{[[c]]}{(z-([[z0]]))^{3}} \), and let \( C \) be a positively oriented simple closed contour enclosing only the point \( z=[[z0]] \). Compute \( \displaystyle\oint_{C} f(z)\,dz \).
solution: Only the SIMPLE pole term \( \dfrac{[[a]]}{z-[[z0]]} \) contributes to the contour integral: the residue of \( f \) at \( z=[[z0]] \) is the coefficient of the \( (z-z0)^{-1} \) term in its Laurent expansion, which is \( [[a]] \). The higher-order terms \( \frac{[[b]]}{(z-z0)^{2}} \) and \( \frac{[[c]]}{(z-z0)^{3}} \) have single-valued antiderivatives, so they integrate to \( 0 \) around any closed contour avoiding \( z_0 \). Hence \( \oint_{C} f(z)\,dz = 2\pi i\,([[a]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This wrongly adds the coefficients of the higher-order pole terms, which do not contribute to the integral.
This uses the coefficient of the \( (z-z_0)^{-2} \) term, which integrates to \( 0 \), not \( [[a]] \).
This uses the coefficient of the \( (z-z_0)^{-3} \) term, which integrates to \( 0 \), not \( [[a]] \).
This uses \( \pi i \) instead of \( 2\pi i \) as the residue-theorem constant.
This has the wrong sign (the contour orientation was reversed).
This is off by \( 2\pi i \) from the correct value.
